name: Moving Charges and Magnetism
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Magnetic Force on a Moving Charge
sections.body:
A charge $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences the Lorentz force: $$\vec{F} = q\vec{v}\times\vec{B}, \quad |F| = qvB\sin\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.

Key features:

  • Force is perpendicular to both $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$.
  • Zero when $\vec{v}\parallel\vec{B}$ or $v = 0$.
  • Magnetic force does no work ($\vec{F}\perp\vec{v}$) — speed unchanged, only direction changes.

Combined electric and magnetic force: $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v}\times\vec{B})$. Unit of $B$: tesla (T) = N/(A·m); 1 T = 10⁴ gauss.

sections.heading: 2. Motion in a Magnetic Field
sections.body:
If $\vec{v}\perp\vec{B}$, the magnetic force provides centripetal acceleration; the charge moves in a circle of radius $$r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$$ Period: $T = 2\pi m/(qB)$, frequency $f = qB/(2\pi m)$ — both independent of $v$ (cyclotron frequency).

If $\vec{v}$ has components both parallel and perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, the perpendicular part causes circular motion while the parallel part moves uniformly — combined motion is a helix with pitch $p = v_\parallel T = 2\pi m v_\parallel/(qB)$.

sections.heading: 3. Cyclotron
sections.body:
The cyclotron accelerates positive ions to high energies using a perpendicular magnetic field and an alternating electric field across two D-shaped electrodes (dees). The ion crosses the gap each half-cycle, gaining energy; meanwhile the field bends it into a semicircle inside each dee.

The applied AC frequency must equal the cyclotron frequency: $$f_c = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$$ This resonance condition is independent of speed (as long as $m$ is constant).

Maximum energy: $K_{\max} = q^2 B^2 R^2/(2m)$, where $R$ is the dee radius.

Limitations: relativistic mass increase at high speed breaks resonance; not used for electrons (too light, become relativistic quickly).

sections.heading: 4. Biot-Savart Law
sections.body:
The magnetic field $d\vec{B}$ at point $P$ due to a small current element $I\,d\vec{l}$ at distance $r$: $$d\vec{B} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\dfrac{I\,d\vec{l}\times\hat{r}}{r^2}$$ where $\mu_0 = 4\pi\times 10^{-7}\,\text{T m/A}$ is the permeability of free space.

Magnitude: $dB = (\mu_0/4\pi)(I\,dl\sin\theta)/r^2$, where $\theta$ is between $d\vec{l}$ and $\vec{r}$.

Direction: by right-hand rule (thumb along $I$, fingers curl in $\vec{B}$ direction).

Field of a finite straight wire at perpendicular distance $a$, subtending angles $\phi_1, \phi_2$ at the foot: $B = (\mu_0 I)/(4\pi a)(\sin\phi_1 + \sin\phi_2)$. For an infinite wire: $B = \mu_0 I/(2\pi a)$.

sections.heading: 5. Field of a Circular Loop and Solenoid
sections.body:
Centre of a circular loop of $N$ turns, radius $R$, current $I$: $$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$$ along the axis.

On axis at distance $x$ from centre: $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$. For $x \gg R$: $B \approx \mu_0 N I R^2/(2x^3)$, like a magnetic dipole.

Solenoid (long, with $n$ turns/unit length): inside, the field is nearly uniform along the axis: $$B = \mu_0 n I$$ At the ends, $B = \mu_0 n I/2$. Outside an ideal long solenoid, $B \approx 0$.

Toroid (closed solenoid): inside the toroid, $B = \mu_0 N I/(2\pi r)$, where $N$ is total turns and $r$ the mean radius.

sections.heading: 6. Ampere's Circuital Law
sections.body:
Line integral of $\vec{B}$ around any closed loop equals $\mu_0$ times the net current enclosed: $$\oint \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}$$ Analogous to Gauss's law in electrostatics; useful when symmetry makes $B$ constant along the chosen Amperian loop.

Applications:

  • Long straight wire: cylindrical Amperian loop gives $B = \mu_0 I/(2\pi r)$ outside.
  • Solenoid: rectangular loop gives $B_{\text{inside}} = \mu_0 n I$.
  • Toroid: circular loop inside gives $B = \mu_0 N I/(2\pi r)$.

sections.heading: 7. Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor
sections.body:
A conductor of length $\vec{l}$ carrying current $I$ in field $\vec{B}$ experiences force: $$\vec{F} = I\vec{l}\times\vec{B}, \quad F = BIl\sin\theta$$ Direction by right-hand rule (or Fleming's left-hand rule for force on positive current).

Force per unit length between two parallel wires at distance $d$, carrying $I_1, I_2$: $$\dfrac{F}{l} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}$$ Same direction → attract; opposite → repel. This defines the ampere: 1 A is the current that produces $2\times 10^{-7}$ N/m between two infinite parallel wires 1 m apart.

sections.heading: 8. Torque on a Current Loop and Magnetic Dipole Moment
sections.body:
A planar current loop of area $\vec{A}$ carrying current $I$ in field $\vec{B}$ has magnetic dipole moment $\vec{m} = NI\vec{A}$ (direction by right-hand thumb rule). Unit: A·m².

Torque: $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{m}\times\vec{B}, \quad |\tau| = mB\sin\theta = NIAB\sin\theta$$ Force is zero in a uniform field, but torque tends to align $\vec{m}$ with $\vec{B}$.

Potential energy: $U = -\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B}$.

This is the principle of the moving-coil galvanometer.

sections.heading: 9. Moving Coil Galvanometer
sections.body:
A rectangular coil of $N$ turns, area $A$, suspended in a radial magnetic field $B$ (provided by curved pole pieces and a soft-iron core, so torque is always $NIAB$ regardless of orientation). A spring provides restoring torque proportional to angular deflection $\phi$: $\tau_{\text{restore}} = k\phi$.

At equilibrium: $NIAB = k\phi$, so $$I = \dfrac{k}{NAB}\phi$$ — deflection is proportional to current.

Current sensitivity: $\phi/I = NAB/k$. Voltage sensitivity: $\phi/V = NAB/(kR_g)$.

To convert to ammeter: connect a low-resistance shunt $S$ in parallel: $S = (I_g R_g)/(I - I_g)$. To convert to voltmeter: connect a high resistance $R$ in series: $R = V/I_g - R_g$.

summary: Moving charges feel $\vec{F} = q\vec{v}\times\vec{B}$, executing circular or helical paths. Cyclotron exploits $f_c = qB/(2\pi m)$. Biot-Savart gives field of current elements; Ampere's law $\oint\vec{B}\cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}$ exploits symmetry. A solenoid produces $\mu_0 nI$. Parallel currents attract; current loops have moment $\vec{m} = NI\vec{A}$ and feel torque $\vec{m}\times\vec{B}$ — the basis of the galvanometer.
pitfalls: Magnetic force does NO work — speed of charged particle never changes, only direction.
Cyclotron cannot accelerate electrons (relativistic effects break resonance) or neutrons (no charge).
In Biot-Savart, the cross product means dB = 0 along the wire's own line.
Solenoid has B = μ₀nI inside but B = μ₀nI/2 at the ends; outside an ideal long solenoid, B ≈ 0.
Galvanometer current sensitivity vs voltage sensitivity — increasing N increases both proportionally.
Shunt for ammeter is LOW resistance in PARALLEL; multiplier for voltmeter is HIGH resistance in SERIES.
